nativeRange.habitat: Marshy environments, wet stream margins and seepage swamps along the coastal plain of the southeastern United States (Florida through Texas and Georgia) and similar low-elevation wetlands throughout the Caribbean, Central America, and northern South America

decorPreferences: regular trimming to promote denser bushy growth
open midground placement with room to creep

summary: Mint Charlie is a creeping New World mint-family plant with small round leaves and a distinctive minty scent when crushed or trimmed. One of the few true mint-family plants cultivated fully submerged in aquariums, it tolerates a wide range of water conditions, grows without CO2, and suits both foreground and background roles depending on how it is trimmed.
careNotes:
Mint Charlie (Micromeria brownei, now more widely listed under its current accepted name Clinopodium brownei) is a sprawling perennial herb in the mint family (Lamiaceae), native to marshy environments and wet stream margins across the southeastern United States — from Texas through South Carolina and Florida — and extending into the Caribbean, Mexico, Central America, and northern South America. It is one of the only true mint-family plants regularly cultivated fully submerged in home aquariums, and is notable for releasing a fresh, minty fragrance when its leaves or stems are crushed or trimmed.

Submersed height is highly adaptable: kept trimmed, specimens hold at roughly 7 to 15 cm as a foreground or midground creeper; left to grow under stronger light, stems can reach up to around 50 cm and work well as a background plant. The species is broadly tolerant of water conditions, performing well across pH 5.8–7.6, gH 2–15, and kH 2–10, making it suitable for setups ranging from soft and slightly acidic to moderately hard and slightly alkaline. CO2 supplementation is not required, though adding it tends to produce denser, faster growth.

Mint Charlie is not demanding about substrate and can be grown in plain gravel, though it generally performs better in loosely packed, nutrient-rich substrate paired with a regular liquid-fertilizer routine. Growth rate is medium to fast under typical hobby conditions. The plant spreads horizontally as a creeping stem plant and benefits from regular pruning to encourage a denser, bushier habit; left untrimmed it tends to grow vertically. It adapts well to both submerged and emersed (partially above the waterline) growth, making it a forgiving choice for paludariums and riparium setups.
breedingNotes: Propagation is vegetative and straightforward: snip a healthy stem segment and replant it in the substrate, where it will root within days. Regular trimming of terminal stems also encourages lateral branching and a denser cushion form. Floating cuttings will develop roots that can then be anchored into the substrate. Emersed specimens may produce small two-lipped flowers — pinkish-white to lavender, typical of the mint family — but seed propagation is essentially unknown in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary product page. Provided: temperature 65-78 °F, native range Central and South Florida extending into southern Georgia and Gulf-region waters, easy care, moderate lighting ('does not require high-intensity lighting to do well'), CO2 not required, fast growth, midground-to-background placement, prefers loosely packed nutrient-rich substrate, simple liquid-fertilizer routine recommended, propagated via trimming with regular pruning encouraging denser bushier growth.

additional.name: Aquatic Motiv
additional.notes: Provided numeric values: temperature 75-82 °F, pH 5.8-7.6, tank minimum 15 gallons (~57 L), care level 'very easy', lighting low, CO2 not required, maximum height 20 in (~50 cm), background placement, propagation via stem cuttings replanted in substrate. Note: this page lists native range as 'Asia' — this is a vendor data error and is contradicted by every botanical source; the entry uses the correct New World range from Wikipedia/NatureServe.

additional.name: Aquarium Plants Factory
additional.notes: Cross-checked: family Lamiaceae, origin 'North and South America', lighting tolerance 'low to high', CO2 not required but benefits from supplementation, growth rate medium-to-fast, propagation by stem cuttings, substrate not required, fresh mint scent when crushed or trimmed, small round coin-shaped leaves, adapts well to both submerged and emersed growth. Confirms the current accepted name Clinopodium brownei (former Micromeria brownei).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Clinopodium brownei)
additional.notes: Used for authoritative taxonomy and native range: family Lamiaceae, order Lamiales, perennial herb with sprawling square stems and opposite leaves, bilabiate corolla in pinkish-white to lavender, common name Browne's savory, native range 'marshy environments along the coastal plain of the southeastern United States, specifically from Texas through South Carolina' plus Mexico, Central America, South America, and the West Indies. Conservation status secure (NatureServe). Confirms the species was formerly placed in Micromeria and has been transferred to Clinopodium.
